tags.description: Most famous site in Cousteau Reserve. Cousteau bust (placed January 2004) at 12m depth. Dense coral carpet from 3m; angelfish, parrotfish, turtles, barracudas abundant. Brain, pillar and star corals dominate.
Sheltered pool between two islets. Shallow rocky seabed 4-6m, perfect for beginners and discovery dives.
Geothermal hot springs venting at seabed near Pigeon Island. Thermal vents at ~23m, fault formations, spiny lobsters (langouste royale) in crevices. Depth to 23m.
Shore entry from black volcanic sand beach. Seagrass zone transitions to coral reef. Platforms at 12m and 20-35m. Hawksbill and green turtles common in seagrass zone. Angelfish abundant.

Southern end of Cousteau Reserve, last park site toward Bouillante. Small rocky peaks with multicolored sponges, geothermal vents at 23m basin, up to 13 spiny lobsters (langouste royale) in same hole, frogfish sighted. Night dives reveal sleeping turtles.

Submerged volcanic pinnacle in Saintes Channel. Summit at 15m; walls descend to 300m. Always strong, unpredictable currents (hold the mooring line). FFESSM Level 2/PE40 required. Confirmed species: sea turtles, yellow jacks, angelfish, barracuda, tuna, groupers, moray eels, triggerfish, colorful sponges, soft corals.

Shore entry from reddish-sand volcanic beach at Pointe-Noire. Coral gardens, drop-offs. Operated by Anse Caraïbe Plongée (est. 2000). Small groups max 12. Sea turtles, coral reef fish, moray eels reported.
Sheltered bay under sugarloaf hill, Terre-de-Haut. Sloping reef to 20m. Sea turtles extremely relaxed, nurse sharks under overhangs confirmed. Sea fans, sponges. Night dives available.

tags.seamark:name: La Broumine
tags.validation_source: openstreetmap.org only — no published dive sources found
tags.qa_note: No independent dive guide or operator source found for this wreck. OSM data only. Vessel identity, history, and marine life unconfirmed.

tags.name: Epave de l'Îlet Fortune
tags.description: Metal wreck northeast of Îlet Fortune off Goyave. Vessel identity unknown; referred to locally as Épave de l'Îlet Fortune or Épave de Goyave. Accessible by boat or kayak. Parts visible at surface; dive depth to 16m. No site-specific marine life reports found.

tags.name: Épave de Caret
tags.description: Old sand-dredging vessel west of Îlet Caret, Grand Cul-de-Sac Marin. Deliberately sunk ~1998-2000 as fish concentration device. Hull mostly shallow (2-3m shallowest sections), deepest point ~18m. No penetration — structurally unstable. Fish nursery habitat. Visited on excursions from Sainte-Rose.

tags.name: Pointe à Cabrit
tags.description: Multi-level dive at Îlet à Cabrit entrance to Baie des Saintes. Plateau 2-5m for beginners; drop-off to 20m. La Patate Cabrit pinnacle hosts scorpionfish, rock lobsters, damselfish, trumpetfish, moray eels (confirmed by Tribloo). Garden eels in sandy areas. Always sheltered.

tags.name: Le Sec Pâté
tags.description: OSM-recorded wall dive node in southern Les Saintes. Distinct from the celebrated Sec Pâté pinnacle in the Saintes Channel. Advanced wall dive to 30m. Confirm exact location with Les Saintes operators.

tags.name: La Baleine
tags.description: Canyon and reef dive in Passe de la Baleine, Les Saintes. Two perpendicular canyons, max 22m. Contains archaeological wreck definitively identified as the Anémone (French Navy schooner built Bayonne 1823, sunk 7-8 September 1824 hurricane, all 29 crew lost). Site excavated 2015-2019 by French Ministry of Culture team. Sergeant major fish, damselfish, Caribbean angelfish, pufferfish, royal spiny lobsters confirmed. Site type corrected from wall to reef/canyon — canyons dominate 2-22m.

tags.name: Pointe du Gouvernail
tags.description: West of Terre-de-Bas, Les Saintes. Starts at 4m, descends to 22m. Rich predator activity: barracuda, thazard (Spanish mackerel), large-eyed jacks, horse-eye jacks, trevally. Life abundant from 4m. Strong current possible. Suitable for all levels with experience.

tags.name: L'Aquarium
tags.description: Wall dive in Les Saintes with tunnels and plateaus covered with corals and sponges. Schools of gorettes (French grunts), cardinal fish, angelfish, wrasses confirmed. Impressive underwater architecture.

tags.name: Le Gustavia
tags.description: Cargo vessel built 1950 (PAHL SCHIFFSWERFT & MASCHINENBAU, Germany) as Hamburg; renamed Drochtersen (1963), Géocéanique (1975), Gustavia (1982). 48.32m × 7.6m, 416 tonnes. Damaged by Hurricane Hugo September 1989; decontaminated and deliberately scuttled 1991 as artificial reef. Sits upright at 40m on sandy bottom in Cousteau Reserve, ~1 mile from Malendure. Barracudas, groupers, moray eels confirmed. PE40/Advanced certification required.

tags.name: Le Franjack
tags.description: Former sand-transport vessel (original name Oresund) built 1958 at Danyard Frederikshavns, Denmark. 53.32m × 10.04m, 486 tonnes. Renamed Franjack 1976 (after owner's sons François and Jacky). Arrived Guadeloupe 1983 for SAGUA sand operations. Damaged by Hurricane Hugo 1989. Deliberately scuttled June 9, 1996 at 23-24m on sandy bottom of Cousteau Reserve. Engine room, kitchen and hold accessible. Sponges, bryozoans, anemones, eels, shrimp confirmed. Green turtles rest on wreck at night.

tags.name: L'Augustin Fresnel II
tags.description: Canadian lighthouse tender built 1940s. 53m × 9m, 660 tonnes. Served Mediterranean Lighthouse and Beacon Service; transferred to Guadeloupe 1990, retired 1994. Deliberately scuttled July 9, 2003 in Cousteau Reserve. Currently at 31-33m (to propeller) on sandy bottom. Depth at scuttling was 30m; shifted by Cyclone Dean (2007). Cabins, galley, dining room accessible. Barracuda, jacks, langoustines, moray eels confirmed. Note: a duplicate entry 'Augustin Fresnel Wreck' with incorrect coordinates (lat 16.25, lon -61.53) has been removed.
Les îlets à Frégate (Petit Cul-de-Sac Marin) ont disparu (semble-t-il après le passage du cyclone de 1928) et sont désormais des récifs connus sous le nom Frégate de Haut et Frégate de Bas.
tags.name: Frégate-de-Haut
tags.description: Reef in Petit Cul-de-Sac Marin (nature reserve), former îlet submerged after 1928 hurricane, 5 min by boat from Petit-Bourg. No site-specific dive documentation found beyond OSM and regional sources.
Les îlets à Frégate (Petit Cul-de-Sac Marin) ont disparu (semble-t-il après le passage du cyclone de 1928) et sont désormais des récifs connus sous le nom Frégate de Haut et Frégate de Bas.
tags.name: Frégate-de-Bas

tags.seamark:name: Lynndy
tags.description: Passenger boat sunk 2006 after tropical storm in Anse Mire (Baie des Saintes). 15m depth, ~100m from beach, marked with yellow cross buoys. Minimal coral colonization; reef fish present. Snorkel-accessible from surface. Vessel identity: small passenger boat — no published historical record of vessel name origin found.
